questions.stem: A 48-year-old heavy drinker has jaundice, ascites, and asterixis. ALT 120, AST 290, bilirubin 450, albumin 24, INR 2.9; paracentesis SAAG 14, neutrophils 150; Maddrey score 65. What is the most appropriate specific therapy and its mechanism?
questions.explain: Severe alcoholic hepatitis (AST:ALT >2:1, Maddrey ≥32) is treated with prednisolone to suppress cytokine-mediated injury. The ascitic neutrophil count is <250 (no SBP), so steroids aren't contraindicated. (Lactulose, antibiotics, and diuretics treat complications, not the underlying hepatitis.)

questions.stem: A 54-year-old woman with Sjögren's has 6 months of fatigue and intense pruritus. ALT 45, AST 40, ALP 510, GGT 400, bilirubin 18; US shows no biliary dilation. Which test confirms the diagnosis and what is first-line treatment?
questions.explain: A cholestatic pattern (ALP/GGT up, ALT/AST normal) with fatigue, pruritus, and autoimmune comorbidity in a middle-aged woman = primary biliary cholangitis. AMA confirms it; ursodeoxycholic acid is first-line.

questions.stem: A 60-year-old with HCV cirrhosis vomits bright red blood and is tachycardic/hypotensive. After airway control and fluid/blood resuscitation, which three pharmacologic interventions are given before endoscopy?
questions.explain: Variceal bleeding temporizing trio: octreotide (splanchnic vasoconstriction), ceftriaxone (prophylactic antibiotics reduce SBP/mortality), and an IV PPI (pantoprazole) to cover a possible ulcer — then urgent band ligation.

questions.stem: A 24-year-old has acute hand tremors, new erratic psychiatric behavior, mildly elevated transaminases, and a Coombs-negative hemolytic anemia. What is the pathophysiology and the expected slit-lamp finding?
options: ATP7B mutation → impaired ceruloplasmin and copper overload; Kayser-Fleischer rings
questions.explain: Young age + neuropsychiatric symptoms + Coombs-negative hemolysis = Wilson disease: an ATP7B defect impairs biliary copper excretion and ceruloplasmin synthesis → copper overload, with Kayser-Fleischer rings on slit-lamp. Treat with chelation (penicillamine/trientine) or zinc.

questions.stem: A 55-year-old with MASH cirrhosis has worsening ascites despite a 1.5 g/day sodium diet, on furosemide 40 mg and spironolactone 100 mg daily. Creatinine and potassium are normal. Best next step?
options: Titrate spironolactone to 200 mg and furosemide to 80 mg
questions.explain: With stable renal function and electrolytes, the next step is to up-titrate the dual diuretics (toward the max spironolactone 400 / furosemide 160 mg) before escalating to paracentesis or TIPS.

questions.stem: A 35-year-old woman has fatigue and RUQ pain. ALT 850, AST 700, ALP 140, bilirubin 40. Serology: HBsAg neg, anti-HBs pos, anti-HBc neg, anti-HCV neg, anti-HAV IgM neg. IgG 2.5× normal, anti-SMA positive. HBV status and diagnosis?
options: Immune via natural infection; autoimmune hepatitis
Immune via vaccination; type 1 autoimmune hepatitis
questions.explain: Anti-HBs positive with anti-HBc negative = vaccine-induced immunity. The hepatocellular injury with high IgG and positive anti-SMA confirms type 1 autoimmune hepatitis (treat with steroids + azathioprine).

questions.stem: A 62-year-old cirrhotic with ascites and varices (no recent bleeding) has fever, abdominal pain, and new lethargy. Paracentesis: cloudy fluid, 450 neutrophils/mm³. Diagnosis and how to manage the encephalopathy?
questions.explain: Ascitic neutrophils >250 = spontaneous bacterial peritonitis (treat with IV ceftriaxone). The infection triggered encephalopathy; manage that with lactulose while treating the SBP.

questions.stem: A cirrhotic is oriented to person and place but not time, with sleep-wake inversion and asterixis on wrist extension. What West Haven stage is this?
questions.explain: Disorientation to time, behavioral change, and especially asterixis (which first appears at this point) define West Haven stage 2.

questions.stem: A woman with PSC has RUQ pain, fever, scleral icterus, clay-colored stools, and dark tea-colored urine. What explains the stool and urine changes?
options: Complete CBD obstruction preventing conjugated bilirubin reaching the intestine
Overproduction of urobilinogen by colonic bacteria
questions.explain: CBD obstruction stops conjugated bilirubin reaching the gut → no stercobilinogen → pale stools; conjugated bilirubin spills into blood and is excreted renally → dark urine.

questions.stem: A 20-year-old with depression is obtunded. AST 14,500, ALT 6,200, bilirubin 65, INR 3.1. Most likely diagnosis and syndrome?
questions.explain: Transaminases in the thousands with high bilirubin/INR within days = hyperacute liver failure, most commonly from acetaminophen overdose (treat with N-acetylcysteine).

questions.stem: A 71-year-old with decompensated cirrhosis (MELD-Na 17), well-controlled HIV, hypertension, and a prior variceal bleed reports cutting alcohol to 'one glass of wine a month.' What is her absolute contraindication to transplant?
questions.explain: Transplant requires complete, permanent cessation of alcohol — ongoing use (even 'one glass a month') is an absolute contraindication. Age >70 is only relative; well-controlled HIV and prior variceal bleeding are not contraindications.

questions.stem: A 60-year-old with new ascites has an ascitic albumin of 34 g/L and serum albumin of 36 g/L. What is the SAAG and most likely etiology?
questions.explain: SAAG = 36 − 34 = 2 g/L. A SAAG <11 indicates an exudate (intra-abdominal malignancy or infection), not portal hypertension (which gives a transudate, SAAG ≥11).

questions.stem: A 73-year-old with HCV had band ligation 2 years ago after hematemesis; he currently has no ascites and a normal neuro exam. What clinical stage of cirrhosis is he?
questions.explain: Varices with a history of bleeding (but no ascites/overt failure) defines Stage 3. Ascites or overt liver failure would move him to Stage 4.

questions.stem: A patient has ALP 480 and GGT 350 with ALT 40 and AST 38. Which pattern is this and what does it suggest?
questions.explain: ALP and GGT elevated out of proportion to ALT/AST is a cholestatic pattern, indicating interrupted bile flow (obstruction, PBC, PSC). GGT confirms the ALP is hepatic.

questions.stem: A patient with chronic active hepatitis B (HBsAg positive >6 months, elevated DNA and ALT) needs treatment. Which agent is appropriate first-line?
questions.explain: Chronic active HBV is treated with a nucleos(t)ide analogue (entecavir or tenofovir), often lifelong. DAAs (sofosbuvir/velpatasvir) are for HCV; UDCA is for PBC.

questions.stem: A 50-year-old man has diabetes, arthropathy, bronzed skin, a high ferritin and high transferrin saturation, and is C282Y homozygous. What is the mainstay of treatment?
questions.explain: Hereditary hemochromatosis (C282Y, iron overload — 'bronze diabetes') is treated with therapeutic phlebotomy (target ferritin 50–100); chelation is for those who can't be phlebotomized.

questions.stem: A patient with compensated (Stage 1) cirrhosis asks about cancer screening. What surveillance is recommended?
questions.explain: All cirrhotics — at any stage — get liver ultrasound every 6 months ± AFP for hepatocellular carcinoma, which can arise even in compensated disease.

questions.stem: A cirrhotic with tense ascites undergoes a 6-litre therapeutic paracentesis. What should be co-administered and why?
options: IV albumin to prevent post-paracentesis circulatory dysfunction / renal injury
A bolus of normal saline only
questions.explain: For large-volume paracentesis (>4–5 L), give IV albumin (~100 cc of 25% per 4–5 L removed) to prevent post-paracentesis circulatory dysfunction and acute kidney injury.

questions.stem: A 40-year-old man with ulcerative colitis has a cholestatic LFT pattern and MRCP showing multifocal bile-duct strictures with beading. Which antibody and association fit, and what is the medical treatment?
options: p-ANCA; no disease-modifying drug — manage strictures (linked to IBD)
questions.explain: Multifocal bile-duct strictures (onion-skin fibrosis) in a patient with IBD = primary sclerosing cholangitis, associated with p-ANCA. There is no disease-modifying medical therapy; manage strictures and monitor for cholangiocarcinoma.

questions.stem: Which laboratory value is the earliest blood test indicator of declining liver synthetic function?
questions.explain: A rising INR (from reduced synthesis of clotting factors, especially the short-lived factor VII/II) is the earliest indicator of failing synthetic function — earlier than albumin (longer half-life). Transaminases reflect injury, not function.

cards.prompt: Distinguish the hepatocellular and cholestatic LFT patterns.
cards.answer: Hepatocellular: ALT/AST >> ALP/GGT (hepatocyte injury). Cholestatic: ALP/GGT >> ALT/AST (impaired bile flow). GGT confirms a hepatic source of a raised ALP.

cards.prompt: Where do ALT, AST, ALP, and GGT come from?
cards.answer: ALT is liver-specific; AST is also in muscle/heart/RBCs; ALP is in liver, bone, placenta, gut; GGT comes from biliary epithelium and confirms a hepatic ALP.

cards.prompt: Which tests reflect liver function rather than injury?
cards.answer: Synthetic — INR (earliest indicator of failure) and albumin (low → ascites/edema) — and excretory — bilirubin. Transaminases reflect injury, not function.

cards.prompt: What defines hyperacute liver failure and its commonest cause?
cards.answer: Massive transaminases (often >5000) with high bilirubin/INR within 1 week; most commonly acetaminophen overdose (treat with N-acetylcysteine).

cards.prompt: How is severe alcoholic hepatitis identified and treated?
cards.answer: AST:ALT ratio >2:1 with a Maddrey discriminant function ≥32 indicates high mortality → prednisolone (unless active GI bleeding or uncontrolled infection).

cards.prompt: Interpret HBsAg, anti-HBs, and anti-HBc.
cards.answer: HBsAg = current infection; anti-HBs = immunity (vaccine or past infection); anti-HBc IgM = acute infection. Anti-HBs+ with anti-HBc− means vaccinated.

cards.prompt: How is hepatitis C diagnosed and treated?
cards.answer: Antibody screen, confirmed by HCV RNA PCR. It is curable with direct-acting antivirals (e.g. sofosbuvir/velpatasvir) over 8–12 weeks.

cards.prompt: What markers and treatment define autoimmune hepatitis?
cards.answer: High IgG (>2×), ANA, anti-SMA (type 1) or anti-LKM (type 2), interface hepatitis on biopsy; treat with corticosteroids + azathioprine.

cards.prompt: Contrast PBC and PSC.
cards.answer: PBC: AMA-positive, middle-aged woman, fatigue/pruritus → UDCA. PSC: p-ANCA, onion-skin fibrosis/duct strictures on MRCP, linked to IBD and cholangiocarcinoma → no disease-modifying drug.

cards.prompt: Describe Wilson disease.
cards.answer: ATP7B defect → impaired biliary copper excretion and low ceruloplasmin → copper overload. Young patient with neuropsychiatric symptoms, Coombs-negative hemolysis, and Kayser-Fleischer rings; treat with chelation (penicillamine/trientine) or zinc.

cards.prompt: How does hemochromatosis present and how is it treated?
cards.answer: C282Y mutation, high ferritin/transferrin saturation; 'bronze diabetes', arthropathy, cardiomyopathy. Treat with phlebotomy (target ferritin 50–100), or chelation.

cards.prompt: State the four clinical stages of cirrhosis.
cards.answer: 1: no complications; 2: varices, no bleeding; 3: varices with prior bleeding; 4: ascites and/or overt liver failure. Stages 1–2 compensated, 3–4 decompensated.

cards.prompt: What goes into Child-Pugh vs MELD?
cards.answer: Child-Pugh: INR, albumin, bilirubin + ascites + encephalopathy. MELD: bilirubin, INR, creatinine (MELD-Na adds sodium) — used for transplant prioritization.

cards.prompt: How is the SAAG calculated and interpreted?
cards.answer: SAAG = serum albumin − ascitic albumin. ≥11 g/L = transudate/portal hypertension (cirrhosis, heart failure); <11 g/L = exudate (infection, malignancy).

cards.prompt: How is spontaneous bacterial peritonitis diagnosed and treated?
cards.answer: Ascitic neutrophils >250/mm³; treat empirically with IV ceftriaxone, then secondary prophylaxis with oral norfloxacin.

cards.prompt: What three drugs are given for a variceal bleed before endoscopy?
cards.answer: Octreotide (splanchnic vasoconstriction), prophylactic ceftriaxone (prevents SBP/lowers mortality), and an IV PPI (covers a possible ulcer); then band ligation.

cards.prompt: How is hepatic encephalopathy managed?
cards.answer: Identify and treat the trigger (GI bleed, infection, sedatives, electrolytes), and give lactulose (acidifies the colon, traps and evacuates ammonia) ± rifaximin.

cards.prompt: When should a cirrhotic be referred for and listed for transplant?
cards.answer: Refer at a decompensating complication or MELD-Na ≥12; minimum to be evaluated for listing is MELD-Na >15 or Child-Pugh class B/C.

cards.prompt: The earliest blood marker of failing liver synthetic function is:
Transaminases in the thousands within a week most suggest:
Alcoholic hepatitis classically shows an AST:ALT ratio of:
Which serology indicates vaccine-induced (not infection) immunity to HBV?
The diagnostic antibody for primary biliary cholangitis is:
The slit-lamp finding in Wilson disease is:
A SAAG ≥ 11 g/L indicates:
SBP is diagnosed when ascitic neutrophils exceed:
Asterixis first appears at West Haven encephalopathy stage:
Chronic active hepatitis B is treated with:
Select features of decompensated cirrhosis:
Select triggers of hepatic encephalopathy:
Select appropriate interventions for an acute variceal bleed:
Select ABSOLUTE contraindications to liver transplant:
Select correct disease–marker pairs:

cards.text: SAAG = serum albumin − {{ascitic albumin}}; ≥ {{11}} g/L means portal hypertension (transudate).
cards.explain: <11 g/L points to infection or malignancy.
MELD instead uses bilirubin, INR, and creatinine.

cards.text: Cirrhosis stage {{2}} = varices without bleeding; stage {{3}} = varices with bleeding; stage {{4}} = ascites/overt failure.
Give IV {{albumin}} for every {{4-5}} litres of ascites removed by large-volume paracentesis.
cards.explain: Prevents post-paracentesis circulatory dysfunction / renal injury.

cards.text: All cirrhotics get a liver {{ultrasound}} every {{6}} months ± serum {{AFP}} for HCC.
cards.explain: HCC can occur at any cirrhosis stage.

cards.prompt: Order the acute variceal-bleed management:
Order the management escalation of cirrhotic ascites:
items: Therapeutic paracentesis with IV albumin for tense ascites
TIPS or transplant for refractory ascites

cards.prompt: Order the approach to hepatic encephalopathy:
items: Identify and treat the trigger (bleed, infection, electrolytes)

cards.prompt: Match the autoimmune liver disease to its marker:
Match the disease to its treatment:
Match the cirrhosis complication to its therapy:
Match the drug to its liver use:
How is MASLD/MASH managed and why does it matter?
cards.answer: Weight loss (~10%), dietary change, and coffee; it's often asymptomatic but a major driver of cardiovascular mortality (and can progress to cirrhosis/HCC).

cards.prompt: How does lactulose treat hepatic encephalopathy?
cards.answer: It is metabolized by colonic bacteria to acids that trap ammonia as non-absorbable ammonium, and it induces catharsis to evacuate it — lowering blood ammonia.

cards.prompt: What is TIPS and its main trade-off?
cards.answer: A transjugular intrahepatic portosystemic shunt that lowers portal pressure for refractory variceal bleeding or ascites, but it diverts blood past the liver and increases the risk of hepatic encephalopathy.

cards.prompt: Which commonly-cited conditions are NOT contraindications to transplant?
cards.answer: Well-controlled HIV and a history of variceal bleeding are not contraindications; age >70 is only relative. Active alcohol/drug use is an absolute contraindication.

cards.prompt: How are varices prevented from bleeding?
cards.answer: Primary prophylaxis for large varices: a non-selective beta-blocker (nadolol/propranolol) or band ligation. Secondary prophylaxis (after a bleed): NSBB plus serial band ligation.

guides.title: Week 32 · 1 Liver Assessment & LFT Patterns
guides.html:
LFTs

Liver Assessment & LFT Patterns

Week 32 · GI IV — Liver & Cirrhosis
CPC 2 · Year 1
Study Suite

Hepatocellular vs cholestatic patterns, what each enzyme means, the true markers of liver function, and hyperacute failure.

Two injury patterns

PatternPictureMeans
HepatocellularALT/AST >> ALP/GGTHepatocyte damage (viral, drugs, MASLD, ischemia, autoimmune)
CholestaticALP/GGT >> ALT/ASTInterrupted bile flow (obstruction, PBC/PSC)

Enzyme origins

  • ALT = liver-specific. AST = also muscle/heart/RBCs. ALP = liver, bone, placenta, gut (GGT confirms a hepatic source). GGT = biliary epithelium.
Enzymes are injury, not function
True liver function is reflected by synthetic and excretory markers: INR (low factor II/prothrombin — the earliest indicator of failure), albumin (low → ascites/edema from low oncotic pressure), and bilirubin (excretory). A normal ALT with a high INR/bilirubin and low albumin is a failing liver.

Fibrosis staging

  • Fibroscan (elastography): >13–14 kPa suggests cirrhosis. Fibrotest blood panel >0.75 suggests cirrhosis.
  • Platelets <150 → hypersplenism from portal hypertension — a clue to cirrhosis.

Hyperacute liver failure

Massive transaminases (ALT/AST often >5000, into the thousands) + severe dysfunction (high bilirubin, high INR) within 1 week. The most common cause is acetaminophen overdose (treat with N-acetylcysteine).

ALT/AST up = hepatocellular; ALP/GGT up = cholestatic. INR is the earliest functional marker. Transaminases in the thousands within a week = hyperacute failure, think acetaminophen.

guides.title: Week 32 · 2 Hepatitis: Grading & Viral Disease
guides.html:
Hepatitis

Hepatitis: Grading & Viral Disease

Week 32 · GI IV — Liver & Cirrhosis
CPC 2 · Year 1
Study Suite

Grading by ALT, the alcoholic-hepatitis numbers (AST:ALT, Maddrey, steroids), and viral serology with curative therapy.

Grading by ALT

GradeALTCauses
Mild< 300Viral, MASLD, drugs
Moderate> 300Acute viral, autoimmune, ischemia, Budd-Chiari
Severe> 1000As above (and acetaminophen for the thousands)

Alcoholic hepatitis

The numbers that matter
AST:ALT ratio > 2:1 (ALT often 100–300). Severity by the Maddrey Discriminant Function ≥ 32 = high short-term mortality → treat with prednisolone 40 mg/day (suppresses cytokine-mediated injury). Absolute contraindications to steroids: active GI bleeding or uncontrolled infection. (Pentoxifylline is an alternative TNF modulator.)

Viral hepatitis

VirusKey facts
HAV / HEVRNA, fecal-oral, acute self-limited; diagnose by IgM
HBVDNA, blood/sexual. HBsAg = current infection; anti-HBs = immunity; anti-HBc IgM = acute. Chronic → lifelong nucleos(t)ide (entecavir, tenofovir)
HCVRNA, high chronicity. Antibody screen → confirm with HCV RNA PCR. Curable with direct-acting antivirals (sofosbuvir/velpatasvir, glecaprevir/pibrentasvir) over 8–12 weeks
HBV serology in one line
Anti-HBs positive + anti-HBc negative = vaccinated (immune, never infected). Anti-HBs and anti-HBc positive = immune from past infection. HBsAg positive = currently infected.
AST:ALT >2 + Maddrey ≥32 → prednisolone (unless bleeding/infection). HBV: HBsAg = infected, anti-HBs = immune, anti-HBc IgM = acute. HCV is curable with DAAs.

guides.title: Week 32 · 3 Metabolic, Autoimmune & Genetic Liver Disease
guides.html:
Causes

Metabolic, Autoimmune & Genetic Liver Disease

Week 32 · GI IV — Liver & Cirrhosis
CPC 2 · Year 1
Study Suite

MASLD/MASH, the autoimmune liver diseases by antibody, and the iron/copper/AAT overload syndromes.

MASLD / MASH

Hepatic steatosis associated with diabetes, obesity, dyslipidemia; often asymptomatic but a major cardiovascular-mortality driver. Treat with weight loss (~10%), diet, and coffee.

Autoimmune liver disease — know the antibody

DiseaseMarkerClueTreatment
Autoimmune hepatitis↑IgG (>2×), ANA, anti-SMA (type 1), anti-LKM (type 2)Hepatocellular; interface hepatitisCorticosteroids + azathioprine
Primary biliary cholangitisAMA +, ↑IgMCholestatic; fatigue + pruritus, middle-aged woman, autoimmune comorbidity (Sjögren)Ursodeoxycholic acid (1st); obeticholic acid 2nd
Primary sclerosing cholangitisp-ANCA; "onion-skin" fibrosis, bile-duct strictures on MRCPCholestatic; strongly linked to IBD (and cholangiocarcinoma risk)No disease-modifying drug; manage strictures

Genetic overload syndromes

DiseaseDefect / labsClueTreatment
HemochromatosisC282Y (HFE); ↑ferritin, ↑transferrin saturation"Bronze diabetes", arthropathy, heart failurePhlebotomy (target ferritin 50–100); chelation
Wilson diseaseATP7B; ↓ceruloplasmin, ↑urine copperYoung + neuropsychiatric + Coombs-negative hemolysis; Kayser-Fleischer ringsChelation (penicillamine, trientine) or zinc
α1-antitrypsin deficiencyPiZZ; low A1AT, PAS-positive globulesEmphysema + cirrhosisNo specific liver drug
AIH = anti-SMA/IgG → steroids+azathioprine. PBC = AMA → UDCA. PSC = p-ANCA + IBD + onion-skin. Hemochromatosis = phlebotomy; Wilson = KF rings + chelation/zinc.

guides.title: Week 32 · 4 Cirrhosis: Staging & Prognosis
guides.html:
Staging

Cirrhosis: Staging & Prognosis

Week 32 · GI IV — Liver & Cirrhosis
CPC 2 · Year 1
Study Suite

The four clinical stages keyed to varices/ascites, and the Child-Pugh and MELD scores that drive transplant timing.

Clinical stages

StageDefined by
1Compensated, no complications (asymptomatic)
2Varices present, no bleeding
3Varices with a history of bleeding
4Ascites and/or overt liver failure → strongly consider transplant

Stages 1–2 are compensated; 3–4 are decompensated. (A patient with prior variceal bleed but no ascites/encephalopathy is Stage 3.)

Prognostic scores

ScoreComponentsUse
Child-Pugh-TurcotteINR, Albumin, Bilirubin + Ascites, Encephalopathy ("pour another beer at eleven")Post-surgical mortality / survival (Class A/B/C)
MELD / MELD-NaBilirubin, INR, Creatinine (MELD-Na adds sodium)Transplant prioritization
When to refer for transplant
Refer when a decompensating complication arises or MELD-Na ≥ 12. Minimum criteria to be evaluated for listing: MELD-Na > 15, or Child-Pugh class B or C.
Stages: 1 none, 2 varices, 3 varices+bleed, 4 ascites/failure. Child-Pugh = INR/albumin/bilirubin + ascites/encephalopathy. MELD = bilirubin/INR/creatinine (+Na). Refer at MELD-Na ≥12.

guides.title: Week 32 · 5 Ascites & Spontaneous Bacterial Peritonitis
guides.html:
Fluid

Ascites & Spontaneous Bacterial Peritonitis

Week 32 · GI IV — Liver & Cirrhosis
CPC 2 · Year 1
Study Suite

Why portal hypertension causes ascites, the SAAG that splits the differential, diuretic and paracentesis rules, and SBP.

Mechanism & the SAAG

Ascites is driven by portal hypertension + secondary renal sodium retention. The serum-ascites albumin gradient (SAAG = serum albumin − ascitic albumin) splits the cause:

SAAGProcessCauses
≥ 11 g/LTransudate (portal hypertension)Cirrhosis, heart failure
< 11 g/LExudateInfection or malignancy, pancreatitis

Management

  • Low-sodium diet (<2 g/day) + dual diuretics: spironolactone (100–400 mg) + furosemide (40–160 mg), titrated (~100:40 ratio) keeping potassium/renal function safe.
  • Tense ascites: therapeutic paracentesis; give IV albumin (25%, ~100 cc) per 4–5 L removed to prevent post-paracentesis renal injury.
  • Refractory ascites → consider TIPS (after maximizing diuretics) or transplant.
Spontaneous bacterial peritonitis (SBP)
Suspect with fever, abdominal pain, or new encephalopathy in a cirrhotic with ascites. Diagnose by ascitic neutrophils > 250/mm³. Treat empirically with IV cephalosporin (ceftriaxone); then long-term secondary prophylaxis with oral norfloxacin. (A single high count with neutrophils >250 is SBP; treat before culture returns.)
SAAG ≥11 = portal hypertension (cirrhosis); <11 = infection/malignancy. Spironolactone+furosemide; albumin with large paracentesis. Ascitic neutrophils >250 = SBP → ceftriaxone + later norfloxacin prophylaxis.

guides.title: Week 32 · 6 Variceal Bleeding & Portal Hypertension
guides.html:
Varices

Variceal Bleeding & Portal Hypertension

Week 32 · GI IV — Liver & Cirrhosis
CPC 2 · Year 1
Study Suite

How portal hypertension makes varices, the three drugs before the scope, and primary vs secondary prophylaxis.

Mechanism

Portal hypertension → splanchnic vasodilation and portosystemic collaterals → esophageal/gastric varices. Rupture causes massive upper GI bleeding (hematemesis) in a cirrhotic.

Three drugs before endoscopy
After airway + aggressive fluid/blood resuscitation, give the temporizing trio:
  • Octreotide — splanchnic vasoconstriction, reduces portal inflow.
  • Prophylactic IV antibiotics (ceftriaxone) — reduce SBP and mortality.
  • IV proton pump inhibitor (pantoprazole) — covers a possible bleeding ulcer.
Then urgent endoscopic band ligation.

When banding fails

  • Balloon tamponade (Sengstaken-Blakemore tube) as a bridge, then a TIPS (transjugular intrahepatic portosystemic shunt) — effective but increases hepatic encephalopathy risk.

Prophylaxis

SettingStrategy
Primary (large varices, never bled)Non-selective beta-blocker (nadolol/propranolol) OR band ligation
Secondary (after a bleed)NSBB + serial band ligation
Variceal bleed = octreotide + ceftriaxone + IV PPI, then band ligation; TIPS if refractory. Non-selective beta-blockers (nadolol/propranolol) prevent first and recurrent bleeds.

guides.title: Week 32 · 7 Hepatic Encephalopathy & Other Complications
guides.html:
Brain & more

Hepatic Encephalopathy & Other Complications

Week 32 · GI IV — Liver & Cirrhosis
CPC 2 · Year 1
Study Suite

The West Haven stages, the ammonia-lowering drugs, the cholestatic bilirubin story, and HCC surveillance.

Hepatic encephalopathy

Neurotoxin (ammonia) accumulation. Triggers: GI bleeding, infection (e.g. SBP), sedatives, electrolyte abnormalities, constipation. Always find and treat the trigger.

West Haven stageFindings
1Mild cognitive change, euphoria, sleep-wake reversal
2Disorientation, personality change, asterixis appears
3Severe confusion, somnolence
4Coma
  • Lactulose (acidifies the colon, traps ammonia as ammonium, and induces evacuation) is first-line; add rifaximin (gut-bacteria ammonia production) for recurrence.

Cholestatic jaundice physiology

Pale stool, dark urine
CBD obstruction blocks conjugated bilirubin from reaching the gut → less stercobilinogen → pale (clay) stools; conjugated bilirubin backs up into blood and is excreted renally as urobilinogen → dark urine. Plus pruritus and biliary colic.

Hepatocellular carcinoma surveillance

  • All cirrhotics get biannual (every 6 months) liver ultrasound ± serum AFP. HCC can occur at any cirrhosis stage.
Asterixis appears at West Haven stage 2. Find the trigger (often SBP/GI bleed) + lactulose ± rifaximin. CBD obstruction = pale stool + dark urine. Screen every cirrhotic with US + AFP every 6 months.

guides.title: Week 32 · 8 Liver Transplantation
guides.html:
Transplant

Liver Transplantation

Week 32 · GI IV — Liver & Cirrhosis
CPC 2 · Year 1
Study Suite

When to refer and list, and the absolute vs relative contraindications that get tested.

Indications & timing

  • End-stage liver disease with portal-hypertensive complications (ascites, variceal bleeding, encephalopathy), acute liver failure, selected metabolic disease, or HCC within criteria.
  • Refer when a decompensating complication arises or MELD-Na ≥ 12.
  • Minimum to be evaluated for listing: MELD-Na > 15, or Child-Pugh class B or C.

Absolute contraindications

Hard stops
Uncontrolled bacterial/fungal infection; active non-hepatobiliary malignancy (within ~5 years); advanced non-correctable cardiopulmonary disease; severe pulmonary hypertension (>35 mmHg); and active drug or alcohol use (complete, permanent cessation is required).

Relative contraindications

  • Age >70, noncompliance, severe hypoxemia (not from hepatopulmonary syndrome), psychiatric conditions impairing consent, HCC beyond criteria, cholangiocarcinoma.
  • Well-controlled HIV is NOT a contraindication. A history of variceal bleeding is not a contraindication. Age alone is only relative.

HCC and transplant

Hepatocellular carcinoma can be an indication for transplant when within accepted limits (e.g. Milan criteria: one lesion ≤5 cm, or up to three lesions each ≤3 cm, with no vascular invasion or extrahepatic spread). Such patients often receive MELD exception points to reflect their true risk; HCC beyond criteria is a relative contraindication.

Hepatorenal syndrome

A transplant-urgent complication
Hepatorenal syndrome is functional renal failure from splanchnic vasodilation in advanced cirrhosis (diagnosis of exclusion after stopping diuretics and an albumin challenge). Bridge with albumin + vasoconstrictors (terlipressin/octreotide+midodrine), but the definitive treatment is liver transplantation.

After transplant

Lifelong immunosuppression (typically a calcineurin inhibitor such as tacrolimus, ± an antimetabolite and tapering steroids) — balancing rejection against infection and malignancy risk; adherence is essential (and is why active substance use/noncompliance are barriers).

Refer at MELD-Na ≥12; list at MELD-Na >15 or Child-Pugh B/C. Absolute stop = active alcohol/drug use, uncontrolled infection, recent extrahepatic cancer, severe pulmonary HTN. HCC within Milan criteria is an indication (with exception points); hepatorenal syndrome is cured by transplant. Controlled HIV and prior variceal bleed are fine.

guides.title: Week 32 · 9 Cram Sheet
guides.html:
Night-Before High-Yield

GI IV — Liver & Cirrhosis

Week 32 · GI IV — Liver & Cirrhosis
CPC 2 · Year 1
Study Suite
★ Do-not-miss · first-step reflexes
Variceal bleed → resuscitate + octreotide + ceftriaxone + IV PPI, then urgent band ligation (TIPS if refractory)
Hyperacute liver failure (transaminases in thousands, high INR) → think acetaminophen — give N-acetylcysteine
SBP (ascitic neutrophils >250) → IV ceftriaxone now; often triggers encephalopathy (add lactulose)
Severe alcoholic hepatitis (Maddrey ≥32) → prednisolone (NOT if active GI bleed or uncontrolled infection)
Tense ascites paracentesis → give IV albumin per 4–5 L removed
Transplant → active alcohol/drug use is an absolute contraindication (controlled HIV is fine)

Assessment LFTs

Hepatocellular ALT/AST >> ALP/GGT
Cholestatic ALP/GGT >> ALT/AST
Function INR (earliest), albumin, bilirubin
Cirrhosis clues platelets <150, Fibroscan >13–14 kPa
Hyperacute ALT/AST in thousands → acetaminophen

Hepatitis Hepatitis

Alcoholic AST:ALT >2; Maddrey ≥32 → prednisolone
HBsAg current infection
Anti-HBs+/anti-HBc− vaccinated
Anti-HBc IgM acute HBV
Chronic HBV entecavir / tenofovir
HCV RNA PCR confirms; DAAs cure

Metabolic/genetic Causes

AIH anti-SMA/IgG → steroids + azathioprine
PBC AMA → UDCA
PSC p-ANCA + IBD, onion-skin
Hemochromatosis C282Y → phlebotomy
Wilson ATP7B, KF rings → chelation/zinc
A1AT PiZZ; emphysema + cirrhosis

Cirrhosis staging Staging

Stage 1-4 none / varices / +bleed / ascites-failure
Child-Pugh INR, albumin, bilirubin, ascites, HE
MELD bilirubin, INR, creatinine (+Na)
Refer MELD-Na ≥12
List MELD-Na >15 or Child-Pugh B/C

Ascites & SBP Ascites

SAAG ≥11 portal hypertension (cirrhosis)
SAAG <11 infection / malignancy
Diuretics spironolactone + furosemide
Paracentesis albumin per 4–5 L
SBP neutrophils >250 → ceftriaxone, then norfloxacin

Varices & HE Varices/HE

Bleed trio octreotide + ceftriaxone + IV PPI
Then band ligation; TIPS if refractory
Prophylaxis non-selective beta-blocker
HE trigger bleed, infection, sedatives, electrolytes
Asterixis West Haven stage 2
HE treat lactulose ± rifaximin

HCC & transplant HCC/Tx

HCC screen US ± AFP every 6 months (any stage)
Cholestatic urine/stool dark urine, pale stool
Absolute CI active alcohol/drug, uncontrolled infection
Absolute CI recent extrahepatic cancer, severe pulm HTN
Not a CI controlled HIV, prior variceal bleed
Week 32 · GI IV — Liver & Cirrhosis · cram sheet · cue → answer

facets.contra: Active GI bleeding or uncontrolled infection (in alcoholic hepatitis)

facets.mechanism: Hydrophilic bile acid — slows cholestatic progression
Copper chelator — promotes urinary copper excretion
facets.adverse: Nephrotoxicity, marrow suppression, neuro worsening early

facets.mechanism: Somatostatin analogue — splanchnic vasoconstriction reduces portal inflow
Acidifies colon → traps ammonia as ammonium + catharsis
Non-absorbed gut antibiotic — lowers ammonia-producing bacteria
